children.name: Phoeniculus
children.description: Glossy, dark-plumaged birds with long graduated tails and decurved red bills that they use to probe bark crevices for insects. Wood hoopoes are highly social and cooperative, living in small family groups in which all members help feed the young of the dominant pair. They move through trees in undulating, chattering flocks, often clinging to trunks like nuthatches.

children.name: Phoeniculus purpureus
children.description: The most familiar and widespread wood hoopoe, ranging across most of sub-Saharan Africa in woodlands, savannas, and gardens. Its plumage shows a brilliant metallic sheen of green, violet and blue in good light. Groups maintain year-round territories and engage in noisy, chattering displays at dawn.

children.name: Rhinopomastus
children.description: Slender wood hoopoes with an even longer, more strongly curved bill than Phoeniculus. Scimitarbills are named for the elegant scimitar-like curve of their bills, which they use to probe deep into crevices and under bark for insects. They are less social than typical wood hoopoes, often found in pairs rather than flocks.

children.name: Rhinopomastus aterrimus
children.description: A uniformly dark, almost black wood hoopoe with a prominent glossy blue or green sheen and a sharp, strongly decurved bill. It ranges across the savannas of West and Central Africa from Senegal to Sudan. It forages alone or in pairs, probing bark and dead wood.

children.name: Rhinopomastus cyanomelas
children.description: Found in eastern and southern Africa from Somalia to South Africa, favouring acacia and mopane woodlands. It has a dark glossy plumage with a white stripe on the wing and a diagnostic strongly curved black bill. It is more arboreal than the Green Wood Hoopoe, spending much of its time in the canopy.
